speaker: interviewer
text: I want to back up a little bit. And I want to take you back to Uzbekistan and Wroclaw, and ask you whether you were in a displaced persons camp ever.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean a person camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, were you ever taken to a-- to a camp where you were given medical attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Germany in Muenchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Muenchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I showed you the pictures. And that was the camp what they-- German, they came. And they gave medical for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about what your condition was like at that point in time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My condition was-- I was very sick. I was-- I was all the time in the hospitals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the hospital. In-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was [INAUDIBLE] in-- in Muenchen. It was a hospital in Muenchen. I was in the hospital I think for 8 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of treatments were you getting.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was sick [? Tuberculosis ?]. And the treatment they gave me, they put me-- I remember they put me in a cold water and a hot water. And day time, I-- in the morning, I had fever. In the daytime, not. And in the nighttime, they send me in a sanatorium-like place to give me good food to put like-- a-- I didn't look my age. I looked like a little girl of six, seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skinny. I was 11 year old. I was [? swollen, ?] like. I was very sick. And [? once ?] they operated me there on my appendix.

speaker: interviewer
text: Appendix.

speaker: interviewee
text: Appendix. I was in school. And I-- I passed out. And they took me in there. And the operated me. And the operation didn't go-- they forgot the scissors. Or, they forgot a thread inside. And after the operation, after a week, you have to go down from the bed. And I couldn't go down. And the nurses told-- the doctors told not to keep me in that part. I should have to go to the wash room. And I couldn't. And they told me I'm lazy. I don't want to go down from the bed. And I went down. And I fall down, passed out. And they took me again. They operated. They find out that they forgot a thread inside. That time, I needed to be three months in the hospital with something leaking, taking out the water, the bag, what I had to blood-- the bad blood in the water in inside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, I went back in Rosenheim. After Rosenheim, a month later I became sick. And they took me. And I was sick. They took me in in the hospital. The medical, they gave me a very good one. The German, they tried best for us. Only we didn't get the best food. You know, the leaders, they sold on the black market. They sold the chocolate, and the butters, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Rosenheim, you were in hospital there. And who was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean who?

speaker: interviewer
text: What it a Red Cross Hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was, I think, a Red Cross. I think they were-- they came, and they brought us all kind of chocolate and all kind of good stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got those foods there. But then when you went back--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in hospital, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then when you went back? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you talked a little about reuniting with Meir--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after the war. Can you tell me about how you met and what that was like? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somebody told him, is this your sister-- this [INAUDIBLE] is younger sister, the baby sister? And he thought, that's my older sister. And he said, I saw you with that-- that family, you know. And he said, I can't believe it. How can he-- like, cannot survive, with a family. He came there. And he said, I'm Dimmerman Meir. And I heard that you have-- and they didn't want to give me away, like they didn't want to give it. I was a slave there. And why should they [INAUDIBLE] out money a slave. They didn't want to give me away. And I was so dumb. And I didn't understand what is-- I-- I-- I got lost. Like, I thought they are doing me a favor here keeping me.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the friend of your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because I didn't want to go away. And he said, come. I take it to kibbutz. We will go. Maybe we will find more family [INAUDIBLE]. And I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about that. What was stopping you from going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a feeling to my brother. I forgot it. And I got-- I was very hurt all the time alone. I didn't have nobody to tell me come to eat, or go to sleep, or buy me something. And here, even I didn't understand that time, it was bad. I didn't go to school. If-- they told me they kept me like their child. Well, if I wouldn't be your child, she would give me go to school. I was like a slave there, washing the floors, taking care of the water and painting, [INAUDIBLE] to paint, all [INAUDIBLE], everything I did like a grownup person.

speaker: interviewer
text: But still you didn't want to go with Meir.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared. I was scared. I was scared to tell you the truth. I was scared very much. I was scared for people, even. People told me something, I thought everything, they didn't compare. They wanted to take away my place, you know. My brother came and took me away in nighttime over the window from my bedroom. He took me away. And the train, it brought me to [? Rosene ?]. To that-- and that place was only-- we were 360 kids, survivors from underground, of ghettos. And this, we were all signed there. There are survivors from the ghettos to get better, better food. And in the camp, there were doctors coming, checking us, underfed children who were just borns. They gave better food. They look after us better, like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Meir there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Meir was there. He was from the older one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't have choice. I got used to that. I got looking at him and got to recognize him as my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the way we were both in that kibbutz. And the first, they couldn't take big groups to Israel, because it was not legal. We were eight kids. There were six girls and two boys broughted over to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay friends with any of those other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just like a big, one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still visit with them in Israel any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We are-- when I come to Israel, we are close. And half children in the kibbutz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know Kinneret? You know where-- if you were [INAUDIBLE] in Kinneret with the water and the other side water, they built a kibbutz [INAUDIBLE] our group. This kid went out in the kibbutz. And these kids, what they are-- they have children. They're grandchildren there [INAUDIBLE] already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of their names? Some of the friends that you had, what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Last names? [INAUDIBLE] who?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can't remember the second name.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I can't remember all the-- you know, when I see them--

speaker: interviewer
text: You still maintain a nice, close relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very. We were just brothers and sisters. And we were not strangers. If one was sick, another one came. And he wants to bring him food and to look at-- we were just like brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you some more about the reunion with your mother. How long after you got to Israel did you meet your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already a girl from 13 years old. When my mother came, they brought three mothers, the kibbutz. And when my mother came, I was expecting my mother how much I remember my mother, very beautiful woman. And I always saw my mom with a hat and going-- and black hair. And as she came, she was an old lady, gray hair. I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the other girls couldn't believe it there, that's their mothers. And they told my mother. Looked, which one is my child, she asked? And they said, what is the name for your child? She said, [? Hannah ?] Dimmerman. [INAUDIBLE] they sheared them with the long hair, girl, the black, when she came, want to grab me. And I run away. I didn't want it. I couldn't. What were you thinking and feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- I-- I-- I don't know what to think. I thought she is not. She's dead. And she's standing beside me. She's not alive. I said, I had a beautiful mom. That's not my mom. I-- and I could-- that time, I couldn't speak Yiddish. I spoke only Hebrew. And they gave me mom a room. And the [INAUDIBLE], you know, that [INAUDIBLE] that was looking after us, she came me [INAUDIBLE] and said to me, Edna, you have to change. You-- you have to think it's your mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to go to your mom. Look, see your mom crying? You don't want to go to her in that. And she-- and I went here very far standing. And I asked my mom, would you like all the broccoli, and grapefruit, and oranges to your room, you know? And my mother wanted grapefruit. And she'd ask me for a knife, a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what is a [NON-ENGLISH]. And I thought she want a broom to clean up. There was on the floor a little bit dirt. I came, and I asked for a broom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she asked me, why you need a broom? I said, my mom told me a broom she wanted. I brought a broom. And my mom start laughing. She said, I don't want a [NON-ENGLISH]. [LAUGHS] I want a knife. You know what is a knife? And it didn't take long. It took a month. I learned Yiddish to speak to my mom. So she taught you to speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between you and your mother? When--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. I became so close to my mom. Every night, I couldn't go to sleep not to come in and give her a kiss. And we became very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not like a mom. It's like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you don't have nothing never, you find something, [SOBBING] it's very, very close. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometime I used to go in to sleep with my mom. [SOBBING] And I always told my mom, would you never leave us? What did she say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. I'm going to stay with you. Every night when it was raining, I used to go to my mom sleeping. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When my brother got killed, I went to my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took care of her for six months, helped her stand on her feet again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was working. They gave me a job for the city, kindergarten. And I was working. And I brought all the money. I bought the refrigerator. I bought-- bought the furniture for here, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your reunion with Goldie, when that happened, what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't remember my sister. My sister recognized me, which I didn't remember. I just remember always my mother was talking about-- about Goldie, how smart she is, how beautiful she is and [INAUDIBLE] daughter. And I didn't much remember nothing. The moment when I saw her, I became very warm. I didn't want to leave here. And my mother had one room and a kitchen. And she-- she stayed in a camp. And that camp was very, dirty, and very cold. My-- my husband at that time, he decided-- to-- he was not my husband, he was just a friend-- to take her in with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they live together with your now husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Together all the time we were there. In one room, we had-- with the chesterfield was opening, it was double. And there was a bath, which you put it out, a [? military ?] bath, which we took it out. There was-- believe me, we didn't feel was crowded. We were so happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was very sick. You know? She was pregnant. And she was a [INAUDIBLE] like-- and she couldn't eat nothing. And that time it was food and-- and-- tickets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rationing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rationing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they gave three eggs a week and a half a pound of meat for Friday. And my-- my sister would not survive. And first, is God. And the second is my husband. He was a policeman. And he was working with the Arabs. And he used to bring pails with eggs, and food. And they used to make your ice cream, all kind of things from the eggs. They used sheets to bring it up, eat it, and bring it up. And he used to make again. And when she had a little-- the little daughter, the little girl, then she said it the same thing, first God, and second is my, Borys, brother [? alive. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Goldie come to Israel with a husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard, Baruch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: A survivor, yes, a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's--

speaker: interviewee
text: He died three years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bernard Schweig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard Schweig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Born in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you had one younger brother, Moshe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you reunite with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe. Moshe came after I find my mother. A year later, he came with the younger kids. And they brought him to-- they brought him to us. And they told-- he's too young. You have to be a whole day school, not a half a day and a half. Then they took him away, not far from my kibbutz, in [INAUDIBLE]. And there, it was-- it was a whole day. For three years, it was a whole day, only school. We used to visit. Friday he used to come to us. The most he used to come to us, my mother was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel like you had your family again like when you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Each day was not long enough to be with them, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your war experiences together?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother told me how she survived. I told her how I survived. My brother told how he survived. And we always use to talked every Friday, you know, and always crying. And it was not tissues like that. Instead, have the big-- that was not enough. Every-- every time I used to cry. And we couldn't believe how we could survive something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other people in Israel who were not survivors ask you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of people didn't want to believe. How can of person, a young child survive something like that? Lots of people, they say they can't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just nothing. It make me cold, like, you know? Most, the survivors, they believed. Only people, the Israeli people didn't want-- could not believe how can a child survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a grownup person survivor is OK. A grownup can run. How could you run? Where you know where to run? They ask you, question things like that. Well, you start, and you think yourself, how can I really run? How could I survive? Sometimes you thought it was a dream. I just dreamed everything up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was jealous. And people when they were in camps, even they didn't get too much food. They got once a day sliced bread, or they got soup from potatoes. What do-- you needed to find a potato, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that-- they would water for them. They gave once a day. We didn't have that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to find more out about your relationship with Borys, and when you got married, and your family now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my husband, I fall in love with him. He was [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't have nobody. He didn't have-- he was a single guy. He had only his mother. And they didn't have family. He met me. And my mother was very warm to him and loved him. And he felt-- I hope I'm telling the truth. He felt like family to us. Any time when he came, in a holiday, he came to us. My mother gave him food. And he-- he kept my mom like a mother. When we get to marriage, they want his daughter to marry rich boy. My-- my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brother-in-aw had family [INAUDIBLE]. And their son was very loving. He was a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the Schweig family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they wanted I should marry that doctor. And I didn't feel nothing to him, the doctor. And I loved him very much. And he said, I want to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, well, I-- I understand you want a family. You want everything. But how can I leave my family? I have to-- I'm the only one bread-- bread-- where I bring the bread on the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: He arranged this, too. When he came the day to marry, my mother-- my sister make sure my mother not-- I was too young. And my mother needed to go and sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I married, I was 17. And my mother didn't want to sign. I left a letter. I would run away. And I slept for two weeks in kindergarten where I was working. And my mother didn't know where I am. I think my husband knew it. Right, Borys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My boss come and he signed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- and then my mother said to me, sure, Edna. Let Edna to come. I'm going to sign. And that time I came. And they told me, you should know love is not for everything. You get married, and you're going to find out how poor is not good. And I said, all my life I was not rich. And I don't know what rich is. I wanted love. And I wanted a good husband. And I feel like I'm going to have a good husband. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the [INAUDIBLE] made a very big wedding. I think it was about 400 people. We were married. And a year later, I had my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yocheved, after his mother. His mother was called Yocheved. And in-- in Canada, they called her Eva. And when she came here, they ask your name.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, Yocheved? It was not easy for them to tell them what name you have it. As they told her, they can give you choice to pick a name, or Evelyn or Eva. And she came and asked me, mom, what name should you want it I should take? And she said, Evelyn? I'm not going to remember Evelyn. Eva, I'm going to remember. And that's what they called her, Eva. But her middle name is Yocheved. So how old was Yocheved when you left Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about what happened from the year she was born till you left Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my daughter was born, she was a lucky child. Everything what-- I just dreamed what I needed. My husband brought it for her. It was a bad time. You didn't-- everything was on ticket. Bread was on ticket. Even a piece of furnitures, we couldn't get it. And when she was born, my husband went and bought a beautiful white bed and a beautiful room, even a cover for these-- for the flies, you know. Things you couldn't even dream I would have, I had for my daughter. She was a very good child. And after that, we moved. We were living in Karkur with no lights. He was looking after that police. From 8 o'clock to 11:00 is a [? marker ?] with [INAUDIBLE] lights, you know. And we didn't have even water enough. And every day, my husband went to the kibbutz to bring two pails with milk. And I was bathing my daughter in milk, not the water. [LAUGHS] And she couldn't-- when the water stopped running, because the lights went down. And when the motor stopped, she couldn't fall asleep. She would get used to the motor. [LAUGHS] We used to take her in a jeep. And my husband used to go around in the jeep. She feeled the noise, that when she's sleeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it feel like to have a baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a good feeling. I tell you why. I didn't have my childhood. I didn't have nothing. I didn't have a toy to play. I didn't have nothing. When I had my daughter, I was playing with her. And I went through my own childhood with my daughter, playing with dolls, playing with the wagons, and going school, teaching her all kinds of things. And I thought, I liked it. I missed that. People thinking what you don't have in childhood, you forget. That's not true. You'll never forget you're missing that. You're missing the love from parents. You're missing family. You're missing everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Yocheved gave you some of those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- she was a very good child. When she became seven years old, I gave in a private school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They told, she's at [? high ?] [? IQ, ?] like, she can go in that private school, French, and Hebrew, and Arab. And at that time, my husband left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he leave, go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to Canada, to Winnipeg. He thought-- he suffered in Israel a lot with the climate. He was very skinny. And he couldn't take the climate there. To go back to Poland or to Russia, I would not go with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then he thought he have an auntie here. Then he going to try go to Canada. Canada is the same weather. He came here. And he really-- after he send me-- two months later, he send me a picture. I couldn't believe it. That's my husband. He gained weight. And he felt good here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He didn't have [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't have much where to be. Like, he told you, he's going [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] He came to [INAUDIBLE]. And two weeks later, she put in the suitcase. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I can ever thank Mrs. [INAUDIBLE] and Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. She took him in. And he felt very good and warm there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometime a friend can be more like family. And I can that-- I can never tell thank you for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'd like to find out more about you're married life, and your daughter, and your grandson's in a moment.
